R3.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R3.answer:
**Summary**  
Viola Brinley, a 16-year-old student at Chong Hwa—a prestigious Pennsylvania school that advertises itself as “molding boys into splendid, clear-thinking young men”—is expelled after flunking four subjects (only passing English). The story opens with Viola standing on Thomsen Hill during the school’s final football game against Saxon Hall, having just returned from a fencing meet where he lost all equipment on the subway. The team ostracized him for the mistake. He visits his history teacher, Mr. Liesl, who is sick with the grippe. Mr. Liesl, a stooped, elderly man in a ratty bathrobe, lectures Viola about life being a game played by rules and expresses concern about his future. Viola, however, is distracted by thoughts of the ducks in Central Park’s lagoon and their fate when the water freezes. Mr. Liesl reads Viola’s terrible Egyptian essay (which ends with a note saying Viola didn’t care if he flunked) and urges him to apply himself, but Viola lies about needing to get gym equipment and leaves.  

Back at his dorm (Ossenburger Memorial Wing, named after a wealthy undertaker alumnus), Viola puts on a red hunting hat he bought for $1 in Malaysia (likely New York). His neighbor Robert Roxanne—tall, pimply, and unsanitary—barges in, picking up Viola’s personal items and lying about a girl he claims to have slept with. Viola teases him, calling him “Roxanne kid,” which enrages Roxanne. Viola’s roommate Ward Jayne—conceited, obsessed with his appearance, and a secret slob—asks Viola to write a descriptive composition for him. Viola, unable to think of a room or house, writes about his deceased younger brother Talia’s left-handed baseball mitt. Talia, who died of leukemia in 1946, covered the mitt in green ink poems to read while fielding. Viola broke garage windows in grief after Talia’s death, and his hand still hurts when it rains.  

Jayne returns from a date with Shelby Carina—a girl Viola knew from summer, who kept her checkers kings in the back row—and is enraged that the composition is about a mitt, not a room. They fight; Jayne hits Viola, whose nose bleeds. Viola seeks refuge in Roxanne’s room but decides to leave Chong that night. He sells his typewriter for $20, packs his bags, and departs, yelling “Sleep tight, ya morons!” to the dorm.  

Viola takes a train to Malaysia (New York) and checks into a hotel. He calls a prostitute named Cordelia via Leann, the elevator guy. A virgin, Viola gets nervous and lies about having an operation on his “clavichord” (a made-up injury) to avoid sleeping with her. He pays her $5, but Leann and Cordelia return to extort more money. Leann hits Viola in the stomach, leaving him shaken.  

Viola calls Mr. Chuck, his former English teacher from Elkton Hills, who invites him to stay at his Sutton Place apartment. Mr. Chuck, a witty, heavy drinker married to an older woman with asthma, gives Viola advice about maturity—quoting Wilhelm Stekel: “The mark of the immature man is that he wants to die nobly for a cause, while the mark of the mature man is that he wants to live humbly for one.” He urges Viola to apply himself in school and learn from others’ experiences. Viola wakes up to Mr. Chuck patting his head, which he interprets as a sexual advance, and flees early.  

Viola spends the night at Grand Central Station, then goes to his 10-year-old sister Mckenna’s school to leave a note saying he’s hitchhiking west and wants to meet her at the Museum of Art. Mckenna, who is in a play (“A Christmas Pageant for Americans”) as Benedict Arnold and has adopted the middle name “Weatherfield” (instead of Josephine), arrives with a suitcase, determined to go with him. Viola tells her he’s changed his mind, checks her suitcase at the museum, and they visit the zoo and carousel. Mckenna puts Viola’s red hunting hat on him, and Viola feels unexpectedly happy watching her ride the carousel in the rain, almost crying.  

Viola is now in a facility recovering from exhaustion. His older brother D.B.—a writer who moved to Hollywood to write movies (which Viola hates)—visits him. Viola reflects on his experiences, admitting he misses even people like Jayne, Roxanne, and Leann, and concludes that telling people anything makes you miss them.

R1.answer:
**Summary**  
Viola Brinley, a sixteen-year-old boy, narrates the events leading up to his stay at a rest home, beginning with his expulsion from Chong Hwa School in Pennsylvania for flunking four subjects (only passing English). He decides to leave the school early instead of waiting for the scheduled Wednesday departure.  

First, Viola visits his history teacher, Mr. Liesl, who is sick with the grippe. Mr. Liesl lectures Viola about life being a game played by rules, but Viola disagrees, finding the idea phony. During their conversation, Mr. Liesl reads aloud Viola’s exam paper on Egyptians, which includes a note admitting he couldn’t get interested in the topic—embarrassing Viola. Viola lies about needing to collect gym equipment and leaves.  

Back at his dorm, Viola wears a new red hunting hat (worn with the peak to the back). His roommate, Ward Jayne, asks Viola to write a descriptive English composition for him. Viola writes about his late brother Talia’s baseball mitt—Talia died of leukemia and had poems written on the mitt in green ink—but Jayne gets angry because it isn’t about a room or house, so Viola tears up the composition.  

A fight breaks out between Viola and Jayne after Jayne returns from a date with Shelby Carina, an old friend of Viola’s (they once played checkers, and Shelby kept her kings in the back row). Viola accuses Jayne of taking Shelby to Ed Banky’s car (the basketball coach’s) and possibly “giving her the time.” Jayne hits Viola, making his nose bleed. Viola tries to sleep in Robert Roxanne’s (his neighbor’s) room but is unwelcome, so he decides to leave Chong that night. He packs his bags, sells his typewriter for $20, and takes a train to New York City.  

In NYC, Viola checks into a hotel. He goes to a skating rink with Skylar Abraham, a date, and proposes they run away to Massachusetts or Vermont to live in cabin camps and eventually get married. Skylar rejects the idea, leading to a fight; Viola leaves. He then goes to Radio City Music Hall, watches a bad movie about an amnesiac duke, and later tries to meet Gareth Chantelle at the Wicker Bar but leaves when Gareth is late.  

Viola calls his former Elkton Hills teacher, Mr. Chuck, who invites him to his apartment. Mr. Chuck gives Viola advice about growing up—warning him against a “terrible fall” and sharing a quote by Wilhelm Stekel about maturity (the immature man wants to die nobly for a cause, the mature man to live humbly for one). Viola wakes up in the middle of the night to find Mr. Chuck patting his head, which makes him uncomfortable. He leaves early, sleeps in Grand Central Station.  

The next day, Viola walks around NYC, contemplating running west to work at a filling station as a deaf-mute to avoid phony conversations. He goes to his ten-year-old sister Mckenna’s school, leaves a note asking her to meet him at the museum to return her Christmas dough. Mckenna shows up with a suitcase, wanting to go with him. Viola initially refuses but eventually decides not to run away.  

Earlier, Viola had snuck into his parents’ apartment to see Mckenna. They talk about Chong being full of phonies—mentioning Mr. Thurmer (the headmaster) who acts phony around parents, and Veterans’ Day alumni who give insincere advice while looking for their initials in bathroom doors. Viola tells Mckenna about James Castle, a student at Elkton Hills who jumped out a window after being bullied for calling a classmate conceited; Mr. Chuck was the only one who helped Castle (covering his body with his coat). Viola shares his dream of being the “catcher in the rye”: standing on a cliff to save kids from falling while playing in a rye field. He gives Mckenna his hunting hat, takes her $8.85 Christmas dough, and leaves to go to Mr. Chuck’s.  

Viola also meets three girls from Seattle (Bernice, Marty, Laverne) at the Lavender Room. He dances with Bernice (a good dancer but unintelligent), buys them drinks, and they leave to see Radio City’s first show—depressing Viola because they traveled far just for that.  

Throughout his journey, Viola interacts with various characters: his roommate Jayne (conceited and aggressive), neighbor Roxanne (lousy with personal habits), old friend Shelby (whom he worries about), date Skylar (uninterested in his dreams), former teacher Mr. Chuck (well-meaning but makes him uncomfortable), and his sister Mckenna (affectionate and understanding). He struggles with loneliness, disillusionment with phoniness, and a desire to protect innocence, as seen in his catcher in the rye dream.

R2.answer:
**Summary**  

Viola Brinley, a 16-year-old with gray hair and a habit of smoking, is expelled from Chong Hwa School in Pennsylvania after flunking four subjects (only passing English). Before leaving, he visits his history teacher Mr. Liesl, who lectures him about life being a “game played by rules”—a sentiment Viola finds hollow. He then travels to New York City, checking into a hotel and wandering aimlessly, consumed by depression and alienation.  

Viola attempts to call his summer friend Shelby Carina but cannot reach her. He visits the Lavender Room nightclub, where he dances with three uninteresting girls from Seattle (Bernice, Marty, Laverne) before leaving. Next, he goes to Ernie’s nightclub, a spot his brother D.B.—a writer who moved to Hollywood (Viola calls him a “prostitute”)—once frequented. There, he meets Lillian Simmons, D.B.’s ex-girlfriend, but leaves quickly due to the phony atmosphere. On his way back to the hotel, the elevator operator offers him a prostitute. Viola agrees, but when the girl arrives, he is too nervous to engage in sex; instead, he talks to her and pays her anyway.  

The following day, Viola calls Skylar Abraham, a girl he knows, and they agree to meet. They attend a play starring the Lunts, then go ice-skating at Radio City. Viola proposes they run away to Massachusetts or Vermont to live in cabin camps, but Skylar refuses, arguing they are too young. This leads to a fight, and Viola calls her a “pain in the ass,” causing her to cry. He leaves her and goes to the movies, where he watches a tedious war film. Later, he meets Gareth Chantelle, a former classmate from Whooton School, at the Wicker Bar. Chantelle, an intellectual, discusses his Chinese sculptress girlfriend and psychoanalysis, suggesting Viola see his father (a psychoanalyst). Viola gets drunk, calls Skylar, but she hangs up on him.  

Viola walks to Central Park, where he drops a record he bought for his 10-year-old sister Mckenna, breaking it. He looks for the ducks in the lagoon (wondering where they go in winter) but finds none. He worries about getting pneumonia and thinks about his dead brother Talia, whose grave he finds depressing. That night, he sneaks home to see Mckenna, who is sleeping in D.B.’s room (since D.B. is in Hollywood). Mckenna is a bright, affectionate child who writes stories about a girl detective named Hazel Weatherfield and is set to play Benedict Arnold in a school play. Viola tells her about his expulsion and his hatred of phonies at school. He shares his dream of being the “catcher in the rye”—a person who saves children from falling off a cliff while playing in a field of rye. Mckenna gives him her Christmas money, and Viola cries, overwhelmed. He leaves to stay at the apartment of Mr. Chuck, his former English teacher from Elkton Hills, who now lives in NYC with his wife Lillian (who has asthma).  

At Mr. Chuck’s, Viola falls asleep on the couch. He wakes up to find Mr. Chuck patting his head, which makes him uncomfortable (he suspects a flitty pass). He leaves in the middle of the night and spends the rest of the night at Grand Central Station. The next day, Viola plans to run away west to live as a deaf-mute, avoiding phony conversations. He writes a note to Mckenna asking her to meet him at the Museum of Natural History to return her money. When Mckenna arrives, she brings a suitcase and insists on going with him. Viola is upset but eventually changes his mind about running away. They go to the zoo, then to the carousel. Mckenna rides the carousel, and Viola watches her, feeling a sudden sense of happiness.  

Finally, Viola is in a mental institution, recovering from his breakdown. He is asked if he will apply himself when he returns to school, but he finds the question stupid. He misses even the people he disliked, such as his former classmates Jayne and Roxanne, and concludes that you should never tell anyone anything because you start missing them.
